subject: everyday-science
description: Solar System & the Sun MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: Who discovered the Solar System (heliocentric model)?
mcqs.explanation: The Solar System (heliocentric model) was discovered by Nicolaus Copernicus.

mcqs.q: According to NASA, what is the mass of the Sun?
mcqs.explanation: According to NASA, the mass of the Sun is 1.989 × 10³⁰ kg.

mcqs.q: The Sun and the rest of the solar system formed from a giant, rotating cloud of gas and dust called a:
mcqs.explanation: The Sun and the rest of the solar system formed from a giant, rotating cloud of gas and dust called a solar nebula about 4.6 billion years ago.

mcqs.q: How many years ago did the Sun and solar system form?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun and the rest of the solar system formed about 4.6 billion years ago.

mcqs.q: Approximately what percentage of the solar system's total mass does the Sun contain?
mcqs.explanation: According to NASA, the Sun is the centre of our solar system and makes up 99.8 percent of the mass of the entire solar system.

mcqs.q: How many Earths would be required to fit across the Sun's disk?
mcqs.explanation: One hundred and nine Earths would be required to fit across the Sun's disk.

mcqs.q: How many Earths could fit inside the Sun's volume?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun's interior could hold over 1.3 million Earths.

mcqs.q: The Sun is the most prominent feature in:
mcqs.explanation: The Sun is the most prominent feature in our solar system — the largest object containing about 99.8% of total solar system mass.

mcqs.q: Does the Sun have rings?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun does not have rings — unlike planets such as Saturn.

mcqs.q: According to NASA, the Sun is a ball of gas containing approximately:
mcqs.explanation: According to NASA, the Sun is a ball of gas — 92.1% hydrogen and 7.8% helium.

mcqs.q: The diameter of the Sun is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: The diameter of the Sun is about 864,000 miles (1.392 million km).

mcqs.q: According to NASA, the mean radius of the Sun is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: According to NASA, the mean radius of the Sun is 432,168.6 miles (695,700 km).

mcqs.q: The Sun's equatorial circumference is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: According to NASA, the Sun's equatorial circumference is about 4,370,005.6 km.

mcqs.q: What is the Sun's outer visible layer called?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun's outer visible layer is called the photosphere.

mcqs.q: What is the temperature of the Sun's photosphere?
mcqs.explanation: The photosphere has a temperature of about 5,500°C (10,000°F) — note that the source quotes core ~15 million°C and photosphere ~5,500°C.

mcqs.q: At the Sun's core, the temperature is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: At the Sun's core, the temperature is approximately 15 million °C (27 million °F).

mcqs.q: What classification of star is the Sun?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun is a main-sequence G2V star — also called a Yellow Dwarf.

mcqs.q: According to NASA, the distance of the Sun from the Earth is approximately:
mcqs.explanation: According to NASA, the distance of Sun from Earth is 93 million miles (150 million km).

mcqs.q: The distance between the Earth and the Sun is equivalent to:
mcqs.explanation: The distance between the Earth and the Sun is one Astronomical Unit (AU) ≈ 150 million km.

mcqs.q: How long does it take for light to travel from the Sun to the Earth?
mcqs.explanation: It takes 499.0 seconds (about 8 minutes 19 seconds) for light to travel from the Sun to the Earth.

mcqs.q: The Sun emits which type of radiation primarily?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun emits shortwave radiation because it is extremely hot and has a lot of energy to give off.

mcqs.q: Why does the Sun emit predominantly shortwave radiation?
options: Because it is extremely hot and has high energy
mcqs.explanation: The Sun emits shortwave radiation because it is extremely hot — hotter bodies emit shorter-wavelength radiation (Wien's displacement law).

mcqs.q: What are the inner layers of the Sun (in order)?
mcqs.explanation: The inner layers of the Sun (from centre outward) are the Core, Radiative Zone and Convection Zone.

mcqs.q: What are the outer layers of the Sun (in order)?
mcqs.explanation: The outer layers of the Sun are the Photosphere, the Chromosphere, the Transition Region and the Corona.

mcqs.q: Which is the OUTERMOST layer of the Sun's atmosphere?
mcqs.explanation: The Corona is the outermost layer of the Sun's atmosphere — visible during a total solar eclipse.

mcqs.q: Which is the OUTER VISIBLE layer of the Sun (the surface we see)?
mcqs.explanation: The photosphere is the Sun's outer visible layer — the surface we see, with a mottled appearance due to turbulent eruptions.

mcqs.q: Which type of fusion occurs inside the Sun?
mcqs.explanation: The specific type of fusion that occurs inside the Sun is known as proton-proton fusion — converting hydrogen into helium.

mcqs.q: In proton-proton fusion (in the Sun), hydrogen is fused into:
mcqs.explanation: In proton-proton fusion the Sun fuses hydrogen nuclei (protons) to form helium — the source of the Sun's energy.

mcqs.q: How many layers (inner + outer) does the Sun typically have?
options: 7 (Core, Radiative, Convection, Photosphere, Chromosphere, Transition, Corona)
mcqs.explanation: The Sun has seven layers: 3 inner (Core, Radiative Zone, Convection Zone) + 4 outer (Photosphere, Chromosphere, Transition Region, Corona).

mcqs.q: Which is the HOTTEST region within the Sun?
mcqs.explanation: The Core is the hottest region within the Sun — at about 15 million °C — where proton-proton fusion takes place.

mcqs.q: In which year did Johannes Kepler announce his First Law of planetary motion?
mcqs.explanation: In 1609, Johannes Kepler announced The First Law (planets move in ellipses with the Sun at one focus).

mcqs.q: What does Kepler's First Law of planetary motion state?
options: Planets orbit in perfect circles around Earth
Planets move in ellipses with the Sun at one focus
mcqs.explanation: Kepler's First Law states that planets move in ellipses with the Sun at one focus.

mcqs.q: Comets are best described as:
options: Cosmic snowballs of frozen gases, rock and dust that orbit the Sun
mcqs.explanation: Comets are cosmic snowballs of frozen gases, rock and dust that orbit the Sun — they develop tails as they approach the Sun.

mcqs.q: Asteroids are best described as:
options: Small, rocky objects that orbit the Sun
mcqs.explanation: Asteroids are small, rocky objects that orbit the Sun — most are found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.

mcqs.q: What is the main difference between comets and asteroids?
options: Comets are rocky; asteroids are icy
Comets are icy (frozen gases, rock, dust); asteroids are small rocky bodies
Asteroids orbit the Earth, comets orbit the Sun
mcqs.explanation: Comets are icy bodies (frozen gases, rock and dust) that develop tails when near the Sun, while asteroids are small rocky bodies. Both orbit the Sun.

mcqs.q: Both comets and asteroids primarily orbit:
mcqs.explanation: Both comets and asteroids orbit the Sun.

mcqs.q: An equinox occurs when:
options: The Sun is at its highest point
The Sun is vertically above the Equator
The Earth is closest to the Sun
mcqs.explanation: An equinox occurs when the Sun is vertically above the Equator.

mcqs.q: How many times a year does an equinox occur?
mcqs.explanation: The equinox occurs twice a year — once in March and once in September.

mcqs.q: The March equinox is called:
mcqs.explanation: The equinox in March is called the Vernal or Spring Equinox.

mcqs.q: The September equinox is called:
mcqs.explanation: The September equinox is called the Autumnal or Fall Equinox.

mcqs.q: The Sun shines vertically on the Equator how many times per year?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun shines vertically on the Equator twice a year — at the March (vernal) and September (autumnal) equinoxes.

mcqs.q: Which country is called 'the Land of the Rising Sun'?
mcqs.explanation: Japan is called 'the Land of the Rising Sun' — recognised as such for at least 1,400 years.

mcqs.q: For how long has Japan been recognised as the 'Land of the Rising Sun'?
mcqs.explanation: Japan has been recognised as the Land of the Rising Sun for at least 1,400 years.

mcqs.q: What is a galactic year (also called a cosmic year)?
options: The time Earth takes to orbit the Sun
The duration of time required for the Sun to orbit once around the centre of the Milky Way Galaxy
mcqs.explanation: The galactic year, also known as a cosmic year, is the duration of time required for the Sun to orbit once around the centre of the Milky Way Galaxy.

mcqs.q: The Sun orbits the centre of which galaxy?
mcqs.explanation: The Sun orbits the centre of the Milky Way Galaxy — one full orbit is called a galactic year.
